author: garyrong
content: One exception is manual chain rewind. Geth provides a RPC endpoint `debug_setHead` which allows you to rewind the chain back to arbitrary block. If the target block is prior to the pivot block, the local block will be directly rewound to Genesis and chain will be "stateless". After the call, the snap sync will be re-triggered

author: garyrong
content:
Yes it's true. We need to think about if it makes sense to still support it. In the snap/1, it's very easy to utilize the existing state data locally and state healer will fix the state inconsistency very quickly in this scenario. In snap/2, it's definitely more complicated.

- If the target block is after the pivot block and geth has corresponding reverse diffs available, then state can be reverted with these reverse diffs, no snap sync required;
- If the target block is prior to the pivot block and with no reverse diffs available
    - Shall we terminate the rewind? Any benefits by keeping doing this?
    - If we keep rewinding, the local state should be reset and run the snap/2 again

author: garyrong
content:
> i would assume the EL would only fetch the finalized state
It's not correct. There is no guarantee the last finalized state is still accessible over the network. 

Geth by default keep the states of latest 128 blocks, namely ~25 minutes time window for state sync. If the chain is un-finalized for whatever reason, then it's impossible to sync up to the last finalized state

author: nero_eth
content: today, snap sync takes downloads the state at HEAD-64, without any guarantees that we have finalized.

author: m.kalinin
content: q3: I think the answer is "yes, a node can be tricked" unless block hash is verified for each payload on the chain between `S` and `F`. My answer assumes that block hash trust assumption hasn't changed in Gloas which I am not sure about. Block hash commits to e.g. execution requests which are given to CL and if that commitment isn't validated on the boundary between CL and EL then EL validation of execution requests doesn't matter as the EL block between `S` and `F` may have a different block hash, not the one that CL is aware of. I think this is why we have different sync strategies for CLs today: some of them are capable of verifying block hash on their own, some of them are not and defer that to EL.

author: m.kalinin
content:
So, the situation I am trying to analyze is the following:
`S <- M <- ... <- F`, where `M` is a malicious beacon block which associated with the `block_hash` of existing payload `P'` while content of `M` match the content of payload `P`. EL will fetch `P'` from p2p and import it without a problem as it's a valid EL block, then all blocks built on top of `M` including `F` will be validated as well.

What am I missing here?

author: potuz
content: that the EL could not possibly tell you that F is valid in that case

author: potuz
content: the execution follows P and the hash chain follows P'

author: potuz
content: so F cannot be valid

content: why the execution would not follow the chain rooted at `P'`?

author: potuz
content: So I believe all we need to do is to ascertain that the hash chain matches both sides, this should also cover requests at the same time

author: potuz
content: Execution will follow whatever chain, but if it reaches F then it has followed a chain that reaches F which commits all of its ancestry

author: m.kalinin
content: block hashes of each block rooted at `P'` are enshrined into beacon blocks starting from `M` till the head of the chain. It's an extreme case where malicious block is finalized
